text: Royal Perth Hospital Delivering a Healthy WARPH 09120104 Management of Agitation in Older Patients on Medical and Surgical Wards at RPH This guide refers to patients with persistent behavioural problems such as •	 restlessness,	fidgeting,	jumpiness	 •	 pacing 	 •	 pulling	at	lines	and	tubes	 •	 trying	to	leave	ward	or	hospital 	 •	 continually	trying	to	get	out	of	bed	 •	 shouting,	swearing,	arguing,	insulting	staff 	 •	 talking	to	self	 •	 threatening	or	trying	to	hit,	kick,	bite	or	scratch Behaviour like this is usually due to delirium or dementia. Remember... •	 Delirium	may	indicate	a	serious	underlying	medical	problem	or	medication	side	effect. 	 •	 Agitated	patients	with	delirium	or	dementia	sometimes	injure	staff	or	themselves. 	 •	 Benzodiazepines	usually	make	agitation	worse	in	delirium	and	increase	the	risk	of	falls,	aspiration	etc. This guide is not about managing patients with... •	 Simple	insomnia 	 •	 Simple	threats	to	leave	hospital	against	medical	advice	or	conflict	about	a	specific	issue	 	 •	 Behavioural	problems	due	to	a	psychiatric	disorder	such	as	mania	or	psychosis In	these	situations	provide	counselling	or	discuss	with	a	senior	colleague. Repeat	or If		the	response		is	 inadequate	 Proceed	to	Step	5 1 These medication doses are conservative. Giving higher doses on the first night is unlikely to help. Rely on non-pharmacological measures. If the situation is escalating get advice from a senior colleague. Refer to OPAL (Older persons Psychiatry and Liaison) the next day. Pager 3149 Developed	by	Dr	C	Rhodes,	Dr	C	Slawomirski,	Dr	S	Fenner.	Feedback	to	Stephen.Fenner@health.wa.gov.au		OPAL If withdrawal is suspected •	 Use	diazepam	and	thiamine •	 Do	not	use	antipsychotics •	 Refer	to	Alcohol	Withdrawal	 Scale	(AWS)	guidelines	on	Servio	 website If high risk •	 A	single	room	with	a	companion	 is	more	urgent •	 Proceed	with	medication	as	below •	 Discuss	with	a	senior	colleague Step 1. Review diagnosis and screen for new problems such as •	 Sepsis	(UTI,	RTI,	wound) •	 Hypoxaemia 	•	 Pain •	 Urine	retention	/constipation •	 Medication	toxicity:	esp.	anticholinergics	(e.g.	tricyclics,	oxybutynin,	tiotropium),	 digoxin,	sedatives/opioids,	corticosteroids,	anticonvulsants,	antiparkinsons) • Consider alcohol or benzodiazepine withdrawal 2 Step 2. Assess Risk Consider	potential	for	violence,	pulling	out	tubes,	absconding, interference	with	other	patients,	falls,	accidents	etc. 3 Step 3. Non-Pharmacological Interventions These are firstline management. See delirium guidelines on Servio website •	 Maintain	low	level	sensory	stimulation:	soft	sound	and	lighting •	 Single	room	if	possible •	 Staff	calmly	to	engage,	distract	and	supervise	the	patient	 •	 Avoid	confrontation	e.g.	walk	away,	defer	interventions	or	offer	food	and	drink	 to	diffuse	tension •	 Consider	1:1	companion	 •	 Encourage	family	to	stay	and	assist 4 5 Step 4. Use a low dose of a high potency antipsychotic. These agents are calming without much sedation Haloperidol 0.25	to	0.5	mg	(tab,	liquid,	im)	 4	hourly	prn	max.	2	mg	in	24	hours or Risperidone 0.25	to	0.5	mg	(tablet	or	oro-dispersible	 wafer)	4	hourly	prn	max.	2	mg	in	24	hours Step 5. Use a moderate dose of a low potency antipsychotic. These are more sedating Quetiapine	25	to	50	mg	(tablet)	 4	hourly	prn	max.	100	mg	in	24	hours or Olanzapine 2.5	to	5.0	mg	(tablets	or	oro-dispersible	 wafers)	4	hourly	prn	max.	7.5	mg	in	24	hours If the patient has Parkinsonism skip Step 4 and proceed to Step 5 •	 Use	medication	only	if	patient	 is	distressed	or	there	are	 behavioural	risks •			Avoid	benzodiazepines	because	 they	generally	worsen	delirium	 and	may	cause	falls •			Antipsychotic	medications	are	 not	a	first	line	option	and	should	 be	used	with	caution	because	of	 an	association	with	strokes	and	 adverse	cardiac	events Date of Publication: May 2010 Date of next review: May 2012
